caseTitle: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
judgeCName: Kania CJ, Fazl Ali, Patanjali Sastri, Mehr Chand Mahajan, Mukherjea, Das JJ.
headnoteText: First major case interpreting fundamental rights — Article 21 and preventive detention under Article 22
ratiDecidendi: Article 21 protects personal liberty only against deprivation not in accordance with 'procedure established by law.' Each fundamental right is independent — violation of one does not invalidate a law under another. Preventive Detention Act was valid as it followed procedure established by law.
fullJudgmentText: The petitioner challenged the Preventive Detention Act, 1950 as violating Articles 13, 19, 21, and 22. The Supreme Court by majority held that 'procedure established by law' in Article 21 means any procedure laid down by a validly enacted law, not necessarily a just, fair, or reasonable procedure. Each Article in Part III is a self-contained code. This narrow interpretation was later overruled by Maneka Gandhi (1978).

caseTitle: State of Madras v. Champakam Dorairajan
judgeCName: Kania CJ, Fazl Ali, Patanjali Sastri, Mukherjea, Das, Chandrasekhara Aiyar, Bose JJ.
headnoteText: Fundamental rights prevail over directive principles — led to First Amendment
ratiDecidendi: Fundamental Rights under Part III are enforceable and take precedence over Directive Principles under Part IV. A communal G.O. reserving seats in educational institutions on the basis of caste and religion violated Article 29(2). This led to the First Constitutional Amendment inserting Article 15(4).
fullJudgmentText: The Court struck down the communal government order of Madras providing reservation in educational institutions on the basis of religion and caste. It held that Directive Principles cannot override Fundamental Rights. This decision prompted the First Amendment (1951) adding clause (4) to Article 15, enabling special provisions for socially and educationally backward classes.

caseTitle: State of West Bengal v. Anwar Ali Sarkar
judgeCName: Patanjali Sastri CJ, Mukherjea, Das, Aiyar, Bose, Hasan, Bhagwati JJ.
headnoteText: Classification test for Article 14 — reasonable classification must have rational nexus with the object of law
ratiDecidendi: Article 14 forbids class legislation but permits reasonable classification. Classification must be founded on an intelligible differentia which distinguishes persons grouped together from those left out, and the differentia must have a rational relation to the object sought to be achieved by the law.
fullJudgmentText: The West Bengal Special Courts Act allowing Government to refer cases to special courts with abbreviated procedure was challenged. The Court laid down the twin test for valid classification under Article 14: (1) intelligible differentia, and (2) rational nexus between the differentia and the object of the statute. The Act was struck down as it gave unfettered discretion to Government without any guiding principle for classification.

caseTitle: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
judgeCName: Ahmadi, Verma, Ramaswamy, Agrawal, Jeevan Reddy, B.P. Jeevan Reddy, S.C. Agrawal, Yogeshwar Dayal, G.N. Ray JJ.
headnoteText: President's Rule under Article 356 is subject to judicial review — secularism is basic structure
ratiDecidendi: Proclamation under Article 356 is subject to judicial review. The power is not absolute and must be exercised on relevant material showing constitutional breakdown in the State. Secularism is a basic feature of the Constitution. Misuse of Article 356 for political purposes is unconstitutional.
fullJudgmentText: A 9-judge bench held that Article 356 is an extreme power to be used sparingly and as a last resort. The President's satisfaction must be based on relevant material. Court can examine whether the proclamation was issued on the basis of relevant material or was a colourable exercise of power. The Court affirmed secularism as part of the basic structure and held that a State government pursuing anti-secular policies provides ground for action under Article 356.

caseTitle: Golaknath v. State of Punjab
judgeCName: Subba Rao CJ, Wanchoo, Hidayatullah, Shah, Sikri, Shelat, Vaidialingam, Bachawat, Ramaswami, Mitter, Hegde JJ.
headnoteText: Parliament cannot amend fundamental rights — later modified by Kesavananda Bharati
ratiDecidendi: Fundamental rights are transcendental and immutable. Parliament has no power under Article 368 to amend Part III (Fundamental Rights) of the Constitution. Article 368 merely provides procedure for amendment, not power to amend. Constitutional amendments are 'law' within Article 13.
fullJudgmentText: An 11-judge bench by 6:5 majority held that Parliament cannot amend fundamental rights. The Court applied prospective overruling — past amendments were valid but future amendments to Part III were prohibited. This decision was effectively overruled by the 24th Constitutional Amendment and the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973).

caseTitle: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
judgeCName: Sikri CJ, Shelat, Grover, Hegde, Mukherjea, Jaganmohan Reddy, Palekar, Khanna, Mathew, Beg, Dwivedi, Chandrachud, Ray JJ.
headnoteText: Basic Structure Doctrine — Parliament can amend Constitution but cannot alter its basic structure
ratiDecidendi: Parliament has wide powers of amendment under Article 368 including power to amend fundamental rights, but this power does not extend to altering the basic structure or framework of the Constitution. Basic structure includes: supremacy of the Constitution, republican and democratic form of government, secular character, separation of powers, federal character, sovereignty and integrity of India, dignity of the individual, unity of the nation, mandate to build a welfare state, and judicial review.
fullJudgmentText: The largest-ever Constitution bench of 13 judges decided by 7:6 majority that while Parliament's amending power under Article 368 is wide (overruling Golaknath), it is not unlimited. The Court propounded the 'basic structure doctrine' — certain fundamental features of the Constitution form its identity and cannot be destroyed by amendment. Justice H.R. Khanna's decisive opinion held that the power of amendment cannot be used to damage or destroy the identity and basic structure.

caseTitle: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla
judgeCName: Ray CJ, Khanna, Beg, Chandrachud, Bhagwati JJ.
headnoteText: Habeas corpus during Emergency — later overruled by Puttaswamy (2017)
ratiDecidendi: By 4:1 majority (Justice Khanna dissenting), right to move any court for enforcement of Article 21 stands suspended during Emergency under Presidential Order under Article 359. No person has locus standi to challenge detention during Emergency. Justice Khanna's historic dissent: 'Life and liberty are not gifts of the State.'
fullJudgmentText: During the 1975 Emergency, habeas corpus petitions were filed by persons detained under MISA. The majority held that during Emergency, when Article 21 is suspended under Article 359, no person can approach any court for habeas corpus. Justice Khanna's lone dissent — that the right to life exists independent of Article 21 — became the cornerstone of later jurisprudence. This case was expressly overruled in K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017). The 44th Amendment later ensured Article 21 cannot be suspended even during Emergency.

caseTitle: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
judgeCName: Beg CJ, Chandrachud, Bhagwati, Krishna Iyer, Untwalia, Fazal Ali, Kailasam JJ.
headnoteText: Expanded Article 21 — procedure must be fair, just, and reasonable — Articles 14, 19, and 21 form a golden triangle
ratiDecidendi: The 'procedure established by law' under Article 21 must be right, just, and fair — not arbitrary, fanciful, or oppressive. Articles 14, 19, and 21 are not mutually exclusive but form an interconnected 'golden triangle.' Any law depriving personal liberty must satisfy the tests of all three articles. Overruled the narrow interpretation in A.K. Gopalan.
fullJudgmentText: Maneka Gandhi's passport was impounded without giving reasons or hearing. The Court held this violated Article 21. Justice Bhagwati's opinion: 'Article 21 does not merely confer a fundamental right but guards against every form of encroachment on personal liberty.' The procedure must satisfy Article 14 (non-arbitrary) and Article 19 (reasonable restrictions). This decision transformed Article 21 into the most dynamic fundamental right, expanding its scope to include right to livelihood, right to health, right to education, right to clean environment, right to legal aid, right to speedy trial, etc.

caseTitle: Hussainara Khatoon v. Home Secretary, State of Bihar
judgeCName: Bhagwati, Koshal JJ.
headnoteText: Right to speedy trial and free legal aid as fundamental rights under Article 21
ratiDecidendi: Right to speedy trial is a fundamental right implicit in Article 21. Procedure which keeps accused in jail for period longer than the maximum sentence for the alleged offence is clearly unfair and unreasonable. Free legal aid to the poor is an essential ingredient of reasonable, fair, and just procedure under Article 21.
fullJudgmentText: The Court discovered thousands of undertrial prisoners in Bihar jails languishing for periods exceeding the maximum sentence for the offences charged. Many could not afford bail or legal representation. The Court held that speedy trial is a fundamental right; prolonged undertrial detention violates Article 21. The State is constitutionally obligated to provide free legal aid to poor accused persons. This case led to release of thousands of undertrial prisoners and establishment of legal aid mechanisms across India.

caseTitle: S.P. Gupta v. Union of India (First Judges Case)
judgeCName: Bhagwati, Gupta, Tulzapurkar, Pathak, Venkataramiah, Fazal Ali, Desai JJ.
headnoteText: First Judges Case — Executive primacy in judicial appointments — later overruled by Second Judges Case
ratiDecidendi: The word 'consultation' in Article 124 does not mean 'concurrence.' The Chief Justice of India's opinion in judicial appointments is not binding on the Executive. The executive has primacy in judicial appointments. Also established: any member of public acting bona fide can approach court for redressal of grievance (PIL).
fullJudgmentText: The Court held that the transfer of judges was an executive function. Consultation with the CJI does not mean concurrence. This was later overruled by the Second Judges Case (1993) and Third Judges Case (1998) which established the collegium system. The case is also significant for expanding the concept of locus standi and establishing PIL (Public Interest Litigation) as a tool for social justice.

caseTitle: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal
judgeCName: Kuldip Singh, A.S. Anand JJ.
headnoteText: Guidelines to prevent custodial violence — mandatory procedures during arrest and detention
ratiDecidendi: Custodial death is one of the worst forms of crime in a civilized society. The Court laid down 11 mandatory requirements to be followed during arrest and detention: (1) police must bear accurate identification; (2) arrest memo with date, time, and witness signature; (3) right to inform relative/friend of arrest; (4) inform of right to legal counsel; (5) medical examination within 48 hours; (6) copies of arrest memo sent to magistrate; (7) arrested person may meet lawyer during interrogation; (8) police control room to display information; (9) arrested person to be examined for injuries; (10) inspection of interrogation records; (11) diary of arrestee at lock-up.
fullJudgmentText: The Court took notice of widespread custodial violence and deaths in India. Compensation for violation of Article 21 through custodial violence was affirmed. The 11 requirements laid down are enforceable as law under Article 141 and failure to comply amounts to contempt of court and departmental action. These guidelines were later incorporated into CrPC amendments and continue under BNSS.

caseTitle: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak)
judgeCName: Bhagwati CJ, Misra, Ranganath JJ.
headnoteText: Absolute liability for hazardous industries — enterprise engaged in inherently dangerous activity owes absolute duty to community
ratiDecidendi: An enterprise engaged in a hazardous or inherently dangerous industry owes an absolute and non-delegable duty to the community to ensure no harm. This is broader than the English rule of strict liability from Rylands v. Fletcher. The enterprise must compensate affected persons regardless of whether reasonable care was taken. Compensation must be proportional to the magnitude and capacity of the enterprise (deeper pocket principle).
fullJudgmentText: Following the oleum gas leak from Shriram Industries in Delhi (after the Bhopal tragedy), the Court evolved the principle of 'absolute liability' — going beyond strict liability by removing all exceptions. Justice Bhagwati held that in a developing country where economic growth must coexist with safety, industries dealing with hazardous substances have absolute liability. If harm results, they must compensate — no defense of due diligence is available. This became a cornerstone of Indian environmental and tort jurisprudence.

caseTitle: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
judgeCName: Verma CJ, Sujata Manohar, B.N. Kirpal JJ.
headnoteText: Guidelines for prevention of sexual harassment at workplace — led to POSH Act 2013
ratiDecidendi: Sexual harassment at workplace violates fundamental rights under Articles 14, 15, 19(1)(g), and 21. In absence of legislation, the Court laid down binding guidelines (Vishaka Guidelines): every employer must establish Complaints Committee; provide procedures for resolution; ensure no retaliation against complainant; create awareness. These are enforceable as law until suitable legislation is enacted.
fullJudgmentText: Following the brutal gang rape of Bhanwari Devi, a social worker in Rajasthan, the Court took suo motu cognizance. Recognizing the legislative vacuum, the Court exercised power under Article 32 read with Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21 to lay down legally binding guidelines defining sexual harassment and prescribing preventive and remedial measures. These guidelines remained law for 16 years until Parliament enacted the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013 (POSH Act).

caseTitle: Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record Association v. Union of India (Second Judges Case)
judgeCName: Verma, Yogeshwar Dayal, G.N. Ray, Ahmadi, J.S. Verma, M.M. Punchhi, Kuldip Singh, B.P. Jeevan Reddy, S.C. Agrawal JJ.
headnoteText: Collegium system for judicial appointments — CJI's opinion has primacy
ratiDecidendi: In appointment of Supreme Court and High Court judges, the opinion of the Chief Justice of India (in consultation with two senior-most judges) has primacy. The executive cannot override the CJI's recommendation. 'Consultation' effectively means 'concurrence.' This overruled S.P. Gupta (First Judges Case) and established the collegium system.
fullJudgmentText: A 9-judge bench reconsidered the First Judges Case and held that for appointment of judges, the CJI's opinion formed in consultation with his two senior-most colleagues has primacy. The executive's role is limited. The Third Judges Case (1998) expanded the collegium to CJI plus four senior-most judges. This collegium system remains the method of judicial appointments in India, the NJAC (99th Amendment) being struck down in 2015.

caseTitle: I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu
judgeCName: Sabharwal CJ, Sinha, Thakker, Chatterjee, Mathur, Bhan, Balakrishnan, Lakshmanan, Katju JJ.
headnoteText: Laws in Ninth Schedule are subject to judicial review if they violate basic structure
ratiDecidendi: Constitutional amendments including those placing laws in the Ninth Schedule are subject to judicial review on the touchstone of the basic structure doctrine. Laws placed in Ninth Schedule after April 24, 1973 (Kesavananda Bharati date) can be challenged if they violate fundamental rights forming part of the basic structure. Amendments are not immune from basic structure scrutiny merely because they insert laws into the Ninth Schedule.
fullJudgmentText: A 9-judge bench held that Article 31B read with the Ninth Schedule cannot be used as a device to immunize laws from judicial review if such laws damage fundamental rights forming part of the basic structure. Equality (Article 14), freedom of speech (Article 19), and right to life (Article 21) are integral to basic structure. Any law placed in the Ninth Schedule after the Kesavananda Bharati judgment can be tested against these rights.

caseTitle: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
judgeCName: Nariman, Chelameswar JJ.
headnoteText: Section 66A of IT Act struck down — freedom of speech in digital age
ratiDecidendi: Section 66A of the Information Technology Act, 2000 which penalized sending 'offensive messages' through communication services was struck down as unconstitutional for violating Article 19(1)(a) — freedom of speech and expression. The section was vague, overbroad, and had a chilling effect on free speech. Terms like 'grossly offensive,' 'menacing,' and 'annoyance' were subjective and undefined. Section 69A (government power to block websites) and Section 79 (intermediary liability) were upheld with reading down.
fullJudgmentText: The Court held that Section 66A was vague and overbroad — it created offences on undefined terms that could cover any kind of online speech including legitimate political dissent. The Court explained three concepts: discussion, advocacy, and incitement — only the last can be penalized. The section failed all three tests of reasonable restriction under Article 19(2). This landmark decision affirmed free speech protection in the internet era and became a global reference for digital rights.

caseTitle: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
judgeCName: Kehar CJ, Chelameswar, Bobde, Nariman, Sapre, Chandrachud, Abdul Nazeer, Kaul, Sanjay Kishan Kaul JJ.
headnoteText: Right to privacy is a fundamental right protected under Article 21 — overruled ADM Jabalpur
ratiDecidendi: Right to privacy is a constitutionally protected fundamental right under Article 21 as an intrinsic part of life and personal liberty. It encompasses at minimum: (i) bodily integrity, (ii) informational privacy, (iii) privacy of choice (including reproductive choices, food, dress, sexual orientation). The majority expressly overruled ADM Jabalpur (1976). Privacy is not absolute and subject to reasonable restrictions satisfying the triple test: (1) legality — statutory backing, (2) legitimate state aim, (3) proportionality.
fullJudgmentText: A historic 9-judge unanimous bench ruled that privacy is a fundamental right inherent in Articles 14, 19, and 21. Justice Chandrachud's leading opinion declared that privacy includes bodily autonomy, decisional privacy over intimate matters, and informational self-determination. The Court expressly overruled the 8-judge bench in M.P. Sharma (1954) and 6-judge bench in Kharak Singh (1962) which held privacy was not a fundamental right. The decision also overruled ADM Jabalpur's majority. This case has profound implications for data protection (DPDPA 2023), Aadhaar, surveillance, and digital rights.

caseTitle: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
judgeCName: Dipak Misra CJI, Khanwilkar, Nariman, Chandrachud, Indu Malhotra JJ.
headnoteText: Decriminalization of consensual homosexual acts — Section 377 IPC partially struck down
ratiDecidendi: Section 377 IPC insofar as it criminalizes consensual sexual conduct between adults in private is unconstitutional. It violates Articles 14 (equality), 15 (non-discrimination), 19(1)(a) (freedom of expression), and 21 (right to life and personal liberty including dignity and privacy). Sexual orientation is an intrinsic element of individual liberty and cannot be basis for discrimination. History owes an apology to LGBT community.
fullJudgmentText: A Constitution bench of five judges unanimously partially struck down Section 377 IPC (dating from 1860) to the extent it criminalized consensual sexual acts between adults. All five judges wrote separate but concurring opinions. Justice Chandrachud: 'Section 377 is weapon to harass and blackmail LGBT community.' Justice Indu Malhotra: 'History owes an apology to members of the LGBT community for the ostracism and persecution they have suffered.' The decision overruled Suresh Kumar Koushal v. Naz Foundation (2013) and affirmed the right of every individual to sexual autonomy under Article 21.

caseTitle: Joseph Shine v. Union of India
judgeCName: Dipak Misra CJI, Khanwilkar, Nariman, Chandrachud, Indu Malhotra JJ.
headnoteText: Section 497 IPC (adultery) struck down — women cannot be treated as chattel of husbands
ratiDecidendi: Section 497 IPC (adultery) is unconstitutional as it violates Articles 14, 15, and 21. The law treated women as property of their husbands — only the man with whom a married woman had relations could be prosecuted, not the woman herself, and the husband was the aggrieved party. This was manifestly arbitrary and based on a patriarchal conception of marriage. Adultery remains a civil wrong (ground for divorce) but is no longer a criminal offence.
fullJudgmentText: A 5-judge Constitution bench unanimously struck down Section 497 IPC and Section 198(2) CrPC. The Chief Justice held that treating a woman as chattel of her husband is offensive to the dignity of women. Justice Chandrachud: 'Section 497 denies women sexual agency and autonomy.' Justice Indu Malhotra noted that the section was based on the premise that a women is the property of man. While decriminalizing adultery, the Court clarified it remains a valid ground for divorce under matrimonial laws.

caseTitle: Indian Young Lawyers Association v. State of Kerala (Sabarimala Temple Case)
judgeCName: Dipak Misra CJI, Khanwilkar, Nariman, Chandrachud, Indu Malhotra JJ.
headnoteText: Women of all ages can enter Sabarimala Temple — exclusion based on menstruation unconstitutional
ratiDecidendi: The practice of excluding women between ages 10 and 50 from Sabarimala Temple on grounds of menstruation violates Articles 14, 15, 25, and 21. Biological or physiological feature unique to women cannot be a ground for exclusion from worship. Right to worship under Article 25(1) is available to all persons irrespective of gender. The rule of exclusion is not an 'essential religious practice.' Justice Indu Malhotra dissented, upholding the denomination's right to manage religious affairs under Article 26. The case was subsequently referred to a larger bench.
fullJudgmentText: By 4:1 majority, the Court held that the ban on women worshippers of menstruating age was violative of their right to equality, non-discrimination, and freedom of religion. The majority held that patriarchal notions dressed as religious practice cannot be sustained. Justice Chandrachud held that notions of impurity based on menstruation are a form of untouchability. Justice Indu Malhotra, the lone dissenter, held courts should not interfere with essential religious practices of a denomination. The case was later referred to a 9-judge bench to consider broader questions about essential religious practice.

caseTitle: Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar
judgeCName: Chandramauli Kr. Prasad, Pinaki Chandra Ghose JJ.
headnoteText: Mandatory guidelines for arrest in cases with sentence less than 7 years — prevent unnecessary arrests
ratiDecidendi: Arrest is not mandatory for every cognizable offence. For offences punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years, police must follow CrPC Section 41(1)(b)(ii) checklist before arrest. Magistrate must not mechanically authorize detention. Non-compliance results in departmental action against police officer and contempt by magistrate. These guidelines apply specially to Section 498A IPC (cruelty by husband) cases to prevent misuse.
fullJudgmentText: The Court noted rampant misuse of arrest power, particularly in matrimonial cases under Section 498A IPC. Directing compliance with Section 41 CrPC amended in 2009, the Court mandated that police officers satisfy themselves that arrest is necessary on enumerated grounds: to prevent commission of further offence, proper investigation, prevent tampering of evidence, prevent influencing witnesses, or ensure court presence. Failure to comply makes the officer liable for departmental action. Magistrate authorizing detention without checking compliance commits contempt.

caseTitle: Lalita Kumari v. Government of Uttar Pradesh
judgeCName: Dattu CJI, Ganguly, Sathasivam, Lodha, Ibrahim Kalifulla JJ.
headnoteText: FIR registration is mandatory when information of cognizable offence is given — no preliminary inquiry needed
ratiDecidendi: Registration of FIR under Section 154 CrPC (now Section 173 BNSS) is mandatory when information discloses commission of a cognizable offence. Police cannot refuse to register FIR on any ground. No preliminary inquiry is required before registration. For cases where information does not clearly disclose cognizable offence (matrimonial disputes, commercial offences, medical negligence, corruption), a preliminary inquiry not exceeding 7 days may be conducted before FIR registration.
fullJudgmentText: A 5-judge Constitution bench settled the long-standing controversy about whether FIR registration is mandatory or discretionary. The Court held that the use of 'shall' in Section 154 CrPC makes it mandatory. If a cognizable offence is made out, the officer must register FIR. Refusal to register FIR is itself an offence. The Court carved out limited exceptions where the information is vague and does not clearly disclose a cognizable offence — in such cases, a preliminary inquiry (maximum 7 days) is permissible. All FIRs must be uploaded online.

caseTitle: National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) v. Union of India
judgeCName: Radhakrishnan, Sikri JJ.
headnoteText: Transgender persons recognized as 'third gender' — entitled to fundamental rights
ratiDecidendi: Transgender persons have the right to self-identification of their gender as male, female, or third gender. Non-recognition of gender identity violates Articles 14, 15, 16, 19, and 21. Hijras and transgender persons are recognized as third gender entitled to legal recognition. The State and Centre must grant legal recognition of gender identity, provide reservations as socially and educationally backward classes, and take steps for their welfare.
fullJudgmentText: The Court recognized the constitutional rights of transgender persons (Hijras, Kothis, Aravanis, Jogappas, Shiv-Shakthis) as third gender. Justice Radhakrishnan held that gender identity lies at the core of one's personal identity, gender expression, and presentation. The Court directed: legal recognition as third gender, fundamental rights protection, affirmative action including reservations, access to education, health, and employment. This led to the Transgender Persons (Protection of Rights) Act, 2019.

caseTitle: People's Union for Civil Liberties (PUCL) v. Union of India
judgeCName: Sathasivam CJI, Ranjana Prakash Desai JJ.
headnoteText: Right to reject all candidates (NOTA) in elections is a fundamental right
ratiDecidendi: Voters have fundamental right to reject all candidates in an election. The right to vote includes the right not to vote and to express disapproval of all candidates. The Election Commission must provide 'None of the Above' (NOTA) button in EVMs. Secrecy of voting is extended to NOTA votes. This promotes clean politics by enabling voters to express dissatisfaction without revealing their choice.
fullJudgmentText: The Court held that democracy is all about choice, and the voter must be given the opportunity to choose 'none of the above' candidates. Negative voting through NOTA increases democratic participation by giving disenchanted voters a means of expression, maintains secrecy (unlike existing 'Form 17A' method which required voter to go to presiding officer), and eventually compels parties to field better candidates. NOTA was first used in assembly elections in November 2013.

caseTitle: Anuradha Bhasin v. Union of India
judgeCName: Ramana, Subhash Reddy, B.R. Gavai JJ.
headnoteText: Internet shutdowns must pass proportionality test — access to internet is a fundamental right
ratiDecidendi: Freedom of speech and expression through the internet is a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(a). Internet shutdowns (under the Telecom Suspension Rules 2017) must satisfy the test of proportionality. Any order suspending internet must be published, subject to review, and limited in duration. Indefinite suspension of internet is impermissible. Orders must be reviewed by a Review Committee within 5 working days.
fullJudgmentText: Following the prolonged internet shutdown in Jammu & Kashmir after abrogation of Article 370, the Court held that access to internet is protected under Article 19(1)(a) (speech and expression) and Article 19(1)(g) (trade and profession). Restrictions must comply with Article 19(2)/(6) and satisfy the proportionality standard: legitimate aim, suitability, necessity, and proportionality stricto sensu. The Court directed publication of all suspension orders and periodic review. While not striking down the J&K restriction directly, the Court set constitutional standards for internet shutdowns.

caseTitle: K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (Aadhaar Judgment)
judgeCName: Dipak Misra CJI, Sikri, Khanwilkar, Chandrachud, Bhushan JJ.
headnoteText: Aadhaar Act upheld but mandatory linking with bank accounts and mobile phones struck down
ratiDecidendi: The Aadhaar Act, 2016 (passed as Money Bill) is constitutionally valid. Aadhaar serves legitimate state aim of targeted delivery of subsidies and services to the underprivileged. However: (1) Section 57 enabling private entities to demand Aadhaar struck down, (2) Linking Aadhaar with bank accounts struck down, (3) Linking Aadhaar with mobile phones struck down, (4) Mandatory for school admissions struck down, (5) Authentication data retention beyond 6 months struck down. Aadhaar mandatory only for PAN cards and for receiving government subsidies/benefits.
fullJudgmentText: By 4:1 majority (Justice Chandrachud dissenting), the Court upheld Aadhaar's constitutional validity while striking down several provisions. The majority applied proportionality test: Aadhaar for subsidies passed the test (achieving legitimate aim of preventing leakage with minimal intrusion) but for private entities and bank/mobile linking it failed (excessive surveillance without proportionate benefit). Justice Chandrachud's 548-page dissent called the Act unconstitutional — particularly its passage as a Money Bill. He described the surveillance architecture as creating a 'surveillance state.'

caseTitle: Vineet Narain v. Union of India (Jain Hawala Case)
judgeCName: Verma CJ, Agrawal, Bharucha JJ.
headnoteText: CBI independence — continuing mandamus to ensure investigation of high-level corruption
ratiDecidendi: CBI must be insulated from political interference. CVC (Central Vigilance Commission) must be given statutory status. CBI Director has fixed 2-year tenure and can only be removed with CVC approval. Investigation of cases involving high officials must not be influenced by the executive. The Court used 'continuing mandamus' to monitor investigation of the Jain Hawala Diary case involving politicians.
fullJudgmentText: In the Jain Diary case involving payments to politicians, CBI failed to investigate effectively due to political pressure. The Court took unprecedented steps: monitored investigation through continuing mandamus, directed CBI to report periodically, and laid down guidelines for CBI independence. These guidelines were later incorporated into the CVC Act, 2003 and Delhi Special Police Establishment Act amendments. The case established that rule of law demands that no one is above the law — investigations must proceed regardless of the political status of the accused.

caseTitle: Prakash Singh v. Union of India
judgeCName: Sabharwal CJ, Thakker JJ.
headnoteText: Police reforms — 7 directives to ensure police independence and accountability
ratiDecidendi: The Court issued 7 binding directives for police reform: (1) Constitute State Security Commission for policy and oversight; (2) DGP selection from 3 names recommended by UPSC panel, minimum 2-year tenure; (3) Minimum 2-year tenure for SP and above; (4) Separate investigation from law and order; (5) Establish Police Establishment Board for transfers and postings; (6) Constitute Police Complaints Authority at state and district level; (7) Set up National Security Commission at Centre.
fullJudgmentText: The Court recognized that the police system inherited from colonial era was outdated and needed reform to make it accountable, efficient, and rights-respecting. Despite multiple committee recommendations (National Police Commission 1977-81, Ribeiro Committee, Padmanabhaiah Committee), no State had implemented reforms. The Court issued mandatory directives binding on all States and Union Territories. Compliance has been partial — several States have enacted new Police Acts but full implementation remains incomplete.

caseTitle: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (Mandal Commission Case)
judgeCName: Reddy CJ, Kuldip Singh, Sawant, Sahai, Pandian, Ahmadi, Jeevan Reddy, Thommen, Venkatachalliah JJ.
headnoteText: 27% OBC reservation upheld — total reservation capped at 50% — creamy layer concept introduced
ratiDecidendi: Reservation for Other Backward Classes (OBCs) under Article 16(4) is constitutionally valid. However: (1) Total reservation shall not exceed 50% except in extraordinary situations; (2) 'Creamy layer' (socially advanced persons within backward classes) must be excluded from reservation; (3) Article 16(4) is not an exception to Article 16(1) but a facet of equality; (4) No reservation in promotions under Article 16(4); (5) Backward classes are to be identified based on caste plus economic criteria.
fullJudgmentText: A 9-judge bench by 6:3 upheld the Government's decision to implement the Mandal Commission's recommendation of 27% reservation for OBCs in central government services. The Court established the 50% ceiling (with exceptional flexibility for far-flung areas), introduced the creamy layer exclusion concept (economically well-off within backward classes), and held that caste can be a starting point for identifying social backwardness but is not the sole criterion. The case fundamentally shaped India's affirmative action framework.

caseTitle: Vivek Narayan Sharma v. Union of India (Demonetisation Case)
judgeCName: Abdul Nazeer, B.R. Gavai, A.S. Bopanna, V. Ramasubramanian, B.V. Nagarathna JJ.
headnoteText: Demonetisation of Rs. 500 and Rs. 1000 notes upheld — 4:1 majority — government action had reasonable nexus
ratiDecidendi: The demonetisation notification of 8 November 2016 was a lawful exercise of power under Section 26(2) of the RBI Act. There existed reasonable nexus between the measure and its objectives (curbing black money, counterfeit currency, terror financing). The Court would not sit in appeal over economic policy decisions requiring judicial restraint. Justice Nagarathna dissented: demonetisation should have been through Parliamentary legislation and the RBI's independent recommendation was absent.
fullJudgmentText: By 4:1 majority, the Court upheld demonetisation. The majority applied minimal judicial review to economic policy, holding: (1) Section 26(2) RBI Act permits demonetisation of 'any' series on recommendation of Central Board; (2) consultation between Government and RBI existed (RBI Board approved on 8 Nov 2016 itself); (3) proportionality test satisfied as objectives were legitimate. Justice Nagarathna's dissent: the power under Section 26(2) was meant for specific series, not entire denominations; such a drastic step affecting 86% of currency required Parliamentary sanction; and the RBI recommendation was not independent but government-directed.

caseTitle: Supriyo v. Union of India (Same-Sex Marriage Case)
judgeCName: Chandrachud CJI, Kaul, Ravikumar, Kohli, Narasimha JJ.
headnoteText: No fundamental right to same-sex marriage under current laws — Parliament must legislate — queer couples have rights to cohabitation and mutual support
ratiDecidendi: By 3:2, there is no fundamental right to marry; marriage is a statutory right created by legislation. The Court cannot read same-sex marriage into the Special Marriage Act. However (unanimously): (1) queer persons have constitutional right to choose partners; (2) queer couples entitled to cohabitation, mutual support, joint bank accounts; (3) State cannot discriminate against queer unions; (4) CJI Chandrachud and Justice Kaul (minority) would have allowed civil unions and joint adoption. The matter is left to Parliament.
fullJudgmentText: A 5-judge Constitution bench heard petitions seeking recognition of same-sex marriage. CJI Chandrachud held that while the right to marry is not a fundamental right, the right to form unions and choose partners is protected under Articles 19 and 21. He would have directed the government to recognize civil unions for queer couples and allow joint adoption. The majority (Justices Ravikumar, Kohli, Narasimha) held that the Special Marriage Act was enacted for inter-faith heterosexual marriages and the Court cannot legislate by reading in same-sex marriage. All five judges unanimously recognized the right of queer couples to cohabit and held that the State must not discriminate against them.

caseTitle: Property Owners Association v. State of Maharashtra (Sub-Classification in Reservations)
judgeCName: Chandrachud CJI, Gavai, Viswanathan, Pankaj Mithal, Manoj Misra, Satish Chandra Sharma, Bhat JJ.
headnoteText: States can sub-classify within Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes categories for reservation
ratiDecidendi: By 6:1, States have the power to make sub-classifications within the list of Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes for granting reservation. The Presidential list under Articles 341/342 is not immutable for the purpose of reservation. States can identify more backward groups within SCs/STs and provide preferential treatment. This does not alter the Presidential list itself but only the distribution of benefits within the reserved category. Overruled E.V. Chinnaiah v. State of Andhra Pradesh (2005) which held sub-classification was impermissible.
fullJudgmentText: A 7-judge bench overruled the 5-judge bench in E.V. Chinnaiah. CJI Chandrachud held that treating all SCs/STs as a homogeneous group ignores the reality that some castes within the list face greater disadvantage than others. Sub-classification is a facet of substantive equality under Article 14. Justice Gavai (from Dalit background) provided additional perspective on inter-se inequality within SCs. Justice B.V. Nagarathna dissented, holding that the Presidential list creates a constitutionally homogeneous group and States cannot tinker with it.

caseTitle: Shayara Bano v. Union of India (Triple Talaq Case)
judgeCName: Kehar CJI, Kurian Joseph, Nariman, Lalit, Abdul Nazeer JJ.
headnoteText: Instant Triple Talaq (talaq-e-biddat) is unconstitutional — violates dignity and gender equality
ratiDecidendi: By 3:2, instant triple talaq (talaq-e-biddat) is set aside. Justice Nariman (for himself and Justice Lalit): Triple talaq is manifestly arbitrary under Article 14 and hence unconstitutional; it was never sanctioned by the Quran and is disapproved by other Islamic countries. Justice Kurian Joseph: Triple talaq is not integral to Islamic faith, hence not protected under Article 25. CJI Kehar and Justice Nazeer (minority): Triple talaq is an integral part of religious practice protected under Article 25, but directed Parliament to legislate within 6 months. Parliament subsequently enacted the Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Marriage) Act, 2019.
fullJudgmentText: Shayara Bano challenged instant triple talaq after her husband divorced her by pronouncing 'talaq' thrice. A 5-judge Constitution bench (representing five different religions) struck down the practice. Justice Nariman held it was manifestly arbitrary — giving unilateral, irrevocable power to husband to dissolve marriage instantaneously without attempt at reconciliation. Justice Kurian Joseph held it was not essential religious practice. The Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Marriage) Act, 2019 subsequently criminalized instant triple talaq with up to 3 years imprisonment.

caseTitle: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Taj Trapezium Case)
judgeCName: Kuldip Singh, Faizanuddin JJ.
headnoteText: Protection of Taj Mahal from industrial pollution — application of precautionary principle and polluter pays principle
ratiDecidendi: The precautionary principle and polluter pays principle are part of environmental law of India. Industries in the Taj Trapezium Zone causing pollution must switch to natural gas or relocate. The State must take affirmative action to protect the environment. Right to clean environment is part of the right to life under Article 21. Workers displaced by relocation are entitled to compensation and alternative employment.
fullJudgmentText: The Court noted that the Taj Mahal was threatened by sulphur dioxide emissions from Mathura Refinery and surrounding industries. Applying the precautionary principle (environmental measures should anticipate, prevent, and attack causes of environmental degradation) and polluter pays principle (the polluter bears cost of remediation), the Court directed 292 industries in the Taj Trapezium Zone to switch to CNG or relocate. Workers were entitled to 6 years of wages as compensation. This case became a global reference for judicial activism in environmental protection.

caseTitle: X v. Principal Secretary, Health and Family Welfare Department (Abortion Rights)
judgeCName: Chandrachud, Bopanna, Kohli JJ.
headnoteText: Unmarried women have equal right to abort under MTP Act — marital status cannot be basis for distinction
ratiDecidendi: The Medical Termination of Pregnancy Act (MTP Act) as amended in 2021 permits termination up to 24 weeks for all women regardless of marital status. The meaning of 'woman' in the Act includes unmarried women. Marital rape is recognized as a ground for abortion under the Act — 'rape' includes marital rape for purposes of the MTP Act. Reproductive autonomy is a dimension of personal liberty under Article 21.
fullJudgmentText: An unmarried woman in a consensual relationship sought abortion at 24 weeks after being refused by hospitals. Justice Chandrachud held that the 2021 amendment to Rule 3B of the MTP Rules, which includes 'change of marital status' as a ground, applies equally to unmarried women. The Court held that the law cannot distinguish between married and unmarried women. Significantly, the Court held that 'rape' in the MTP Act must be understood to include marital rape, recognizing that forced sex within marriage is a reality even though marital rape is not criminalized under penal law.

caseTitle: State of Karnataka v. Vishwabharathi House Building Co-operative Society (Mining/Sand Case)
judgeCName: Rajendra Babu, Doraiswamy Raju JJ.
headnoteText: Public trust doctrine — natural resources held in trust by State for public benefit
ratiDecidendi: The State holds natural resources as trustee for the public. The public trust doctrine applies to all natural resources including rivers, seashores, forests, and air. The State cannot transfer public resources to private parties for commercial exploitation when it would harm the public interest. Sustainable development requires balance between development and environmental protection.
fullJudgmentText: The Court applied the public trust doctrine (originating in Roman law and adopted in M.C. Mehta v. Kamal Nath, 1997) to hold that natural resources like sand, minerals, and water bodies are held by the State in trust for the people. Unauthorized mining and extraction violates this trust. The doctrine was used to declare that the government cannot grant mining leases that would destroy river beds, deplete groundwater, or harm the ecology. Environmental impact assessment is mandatory before permitting extraction of natural resources.

caseTitle: Association for Democratic Reforms v. Union of India (Electoral Bonds Case)
judgeCName: Chandrachud CJI, Kaul, Gavai, Pardiwala, Mishra JJ.
headnoteText: Electoral Bonds scheme struck down — violates right to information and Article 19(1)(a)
ratiDecidendi: The Electoral Bond scheme is unconstitutional for violating Article 19(1)(a) — citizens' right to information about political funding. Voter's right to know the source of political funding is a fundamental right. The scheme's anonymity provisions are not proportionate — unlimited anonymous corporate donations are not the least restrictive means to curb black money. SBI directed to disclose all electoral bond purchaser and encashing party information to Election Commission. Finance Act 2017 amendments to RBI Act, Income Tax Act, and Representation of People Act to the extent creating the scheme are unconstitutional.
fullJudgmentText: A unanimous 5-judge bench struck down the Electoral Bond scheme introduced in 2018. CJI Chandrachud held that the right to information about political party funding is essential to the exercise of freedom of expression (Article 19(1)(a)) and the right to make informed choices in elections. The scheme removed the cap on corporate donations, permitted anonymous unlimited donations, and created an asymmetry — the ruling party (through government apparatus) could access donor data while opposition and voters could not. The Court applied the proportionality test: while curbing black money is a legitimate aim, complete anonymity fails the necessity and proportionality tests. SBI was directed to disclose all data to the Election Commission within 3 weeks.
